_note: Static framing copy for Manual + Read. NOT validated against course.schema.json. Body strings are VERBATIM from content-system/anatomy-of-code-course.html. Inline <strong>/<em>/<b> tags are preserved and rendered through raw().

masthead.title:
The Anatomy
of Code
masthead.subtitle: From primitives a junior writes, to a framework an architect governs — the same letters, telescoping all the way down.

one.title: The CODE–CODER Framework
one.body: Four domains hold every digital build at the outer ring (CODE); the engineering letter telescopes into five sub-letters (CODER). The conceptual vocabulary — the anatomy of code itself — now lives inside CODER's C as a collapsible deep-dive, so the framework is the lens you start with and the anatomy is what you zoom into when you need it.

two.title: The Adobe Stack
two.body: The CODE–CODER framework is platform-agnostic; this Part is not. It is the Adobe Experience Cloud as DEPT® practises it — the products an architect on an AEM engagement is expected to place, integrate, and govern. Cloud Manager delivers the platform; Adobe Analytics and CJA measure it; AJO and Campaign activate against it; and LLMO prepares its content for the answer engines that increasingly sit between a brand and its audience.

three.title: The Anatomy of AI-Generated Systems
three.body: Part I teaches how software is built and governed, and Part II maps the Adobe stack it runs on. Part III answers a different question — how should software that AI is writing be structured, governed, reviewed, and evolved? This is where the framework is most differentiated, and where the architect's role changes most. The worked example for this Part is Stalwart, which lives inside CODER's C; this Part covers the principles that apply across Stalwart and any future agentic framework your team writes.

code.heading: C · O · D · E — the whole engagement
code.intro: Four domains hold every digital build. Lose track of any one and the delivery wobbles. Here is the top-level map, then each domain in depth — with the questions a leader should ask and what "done" looks like.

letters.name: Content
letters.desc: Everything the platform manages, displays, stores or publishes — and the governance around it.

letters.name: Operations & Martech
letters.desc: How work moves & which tools support it. Where strategy becomes operational reality.

letters.name: Design & Data
letters.desc: Design is behaviour, not screens. Data is the platform's memory. Hold them together.

letters.name: Engineering
letters.desc: Turns business intent into working software. Opens into its own five-part framework →

c.name: Content
c.intro: Without content there is no experience — so governance starts here, not at launch. The job isn't "write copy"; it's knowing where every piece comes from, how often it moves, who is accountable for it, and how it's structured. Eight dimensions to pin down for each content type:

dims.title: Source
dims.desc: Where it originates — authored, syndicated, a feed, or user-generated.

dims.title: Frequency
dims.desc: How often it changes or needs refreshing — static, periodic, real-time.

dims.title: Asset Management
dims.desc: The DAM: where assets live, formats, usage rights, expiry.

dims.title: Approval
dims.desc: Who signs off, and the gates a piece passes before it publishes.

dims.title: Responsible Parties
dims.desc: Distinct owners for create, approve and publish.

dims.title: Translations
dims.desc: Which locales, and how localization is sourced & kept in sync.

dims.title: Attributes
dims.desc: The metadata fields each type carries, and which are reusable.

asks: What content types exist, and how are they organised into a hierarchy?
Where does each type originate, and how frequently does it change?
Who creates it, who approves it, who publishes it — and which locales?
What is reusable versus unique, and which attributes define each type?
What performs, what converts, and what should be retired?

o.name: Operations & Martech
o.intro: Operations determine how work moves through the organisation; Martech determines which tools support that work — AEM, Analytics, Target, AEP, CRM, CDP, automation. This is where a strategy on a slide becomes a process people actually run.
asks: How does work begin, how does it move, and how is it approved?
Who owns each step — and what is automated versus manual?
What metrics matter, who consumes them, and how often are they reviewed?

d.name: Design & Data
d.intro: Often split, better viewed together. Design is behaviour — journeys, components, accessibility, interaction. Data is what the system understands and remembers — the fuel for analytics, personalization, AI and optimization.

askGroups.label: Design — ask
items: Who are the users, and what are they trying to achieve?
What are the key journeys, and where do people struggle?

askGroups.label: Data — ask
items: What do we collect, and why? Who owns it?
How is it protected, and how is it used downstream?

e.name: Engineering
e.intro: Engineering transforms business intent into working software. Unlike the other three, this letter doesn't just have questions — it has its own five-layer framework. Zoom in below.

coder.heading: Engineering expands into C · O · D · E · R
coder.intro: Zoom into the E of CODE and a second framework unfolds — this time with five letters. It's how an engineering lead keeps the build itself honest.

letters.name: Code
letters.desc: FE · BE · integration · project management. This is where all of Part I lives.

letters.name: Optimization & Quality
letters.desc: Performance, testing, QA — making it fast and making it correct.

letters.name: Deployment & Docs
letters.desc: Shipping it, plus authoring, documentation & the build/support layer.

letters.name: External Integrations
letters.desc: Third parties, APIs, partner systems — engagement beyond your own walls.

letters.name: Release Management
letters.desc: Versioning, rollout, cadence — the discipline of when things go live.

asks.html: Code — Are models, functions and events identified and documented? (Part I is the deep-dive.)
Optimization & Quality — Are performance targets set and quality gates established? Will it hold at 100 users — and at 100,000?
Deployment — Is it automated? Can we roll back? Are environments aligned?
External Integrations — What systems are involved, what happens when they fail, and who owns them?
Release Management — Is release readiness established, is hypercare planned, is success measurable?

coder.blockquote: Most digital failures are release failures, not engineering failures. Change reaching production safely is its own discipline — that's why R exists.

o.heading: Optimization & Quality 101
o.body: Inside CODER, the O letter holds three disciplines that decide whether what you built actually deserves to ship: testing, performance, and accessibility. PMs treat them as separate phases at the end of a project; architects treat them as continuous constraints from day one. This deep-dive covers each — what they ask, where they sit in the engineering loop, and which numbers the team must own.

e.heading: External Integrations 101
e.body: The E of CODER is what happens at the perimeter — every system, vendor and API outside your own walls that your build depends on. Most production outages don't originate in your code; they originate in someone else's, propagated through an integration that wasn't hardened against their failure. This deep-dive covers the topology, the auth menu, the worked examples (Google Maps, Google OAuth, WhatsApp, payments, logistics), and the failure modes that decide whether your perimeter holds.

r.heading: Release Management 101
r.body: The R of CODER is the discipline that decides when things go live — and equally, what gets rolled back when they shouldn't have. Most digital outages aren't engineering failures; they're release failures. This deep-dive starts with Git (where every release actually originates), then covers the branching strategy, the PR mechanics, promotion gates, and versioning.

nest.heading: How it all nests
nest.intro: Here's the elegant part. The framework telescopes — the same letter-logic at every altitude:
nest.blockquote: CODE is the programme. Its E is CODER. CODER's C is the Anatomy of Code. The same questions — when, what, how, where — asked at three altitudes.

review.heading: The Executive Review Model
review.intro: Every digital initiative should be run through CODE–CODER before it's called ready. Eight cards, three questions each — a ten-minute gate that surfaces the gaps a status report hides. Tick a box only when you can point to the evidence.
review.blockquote: CODE tells leaders what must be delivered. CODER tells them how it will be. The review is where both get checked in the same sitting.

watch.heading: Who watches what
watch.intro: A review surfaces gaps; ownership closes them. Here's the practical split at each node — what the architect watches for integrity, and what the PM keeps a tab on for delivery. Product Owners and Delivery Leads read down both columns.

rows.architect: Content model, taxonomy, hierarchy, localization & SEO integrity
rows.pm: Sources, owners, approval & translation readiness; refresh cadence; sign-offs
Tooling access, vendor coordination, launch checklist

rows.architect: UX consistency, data sources behind decisions
The Part I anatomy: patterns, events, structure
Performance budgets, test coverage, quality gates
rows.pm: QA cycles, defect burndown, acceptance criteria

rows.architect: API contracts, failure modes, third-party risk

watch.blockquote: Run the table at every checkpoint. If a cell is empty, that's your next risk — not a gap in the plan, a hole in it.
